demographics.living_situation: lives at home with spouse, who is present and made the call

opening_complaint.verbatim_patient_text: (Spouse on the call) He keeps bursting into tears he can't stop — he says he's not even sad, it just comes over him out of nowhere and won't stop. It's been going on for two days and we're both exhausted and don't know what's wrong.
opening_complaint.stated_reason_for_presenting_today: The crying spells have been happening again and again over the last 48 hours, set off by the smallest things, and he can't switch them off — the couple are distressed and want to know what is going on and what to do.

symptom_narrative.duration: 48 hours, coming in repeated short episodes
symptom_narrative.character: sudden bursts of uncontrollable crying that come on out of nowhere; between the bursts he seems completely back to his normal self
symptom_narrative.severity_patient_description: the spouse says the episodes are frequent and very distressing for both of them, and he is very embarrassed by them
symptom_narrative.timing_pattern: comes and goes in sudden episodes with a completely normal calm state in between
symptom_narrative.what_makes_it_better: nothing they have tried stops an episode once it starts; it passes on its own after a short while
symptom_narrative.what_makes_it_worse: even tiny, unrelated things seem to set it off — the spouse says just asking him if he'd like a cup of tea can trigger one
symptom_narrative.associated_symptoms_volunteered: he insists he does not actually feel sad or upset when it happens; his speech is a little slurred as it usually is, and he occasionally has trouble swallowing
symptom_narrative.functional_impact: he is getting around the house fine as always, but has stopped going out because he is so embarrassed about crying in front of people

past_medical_history.condition_as_patient_states: he's had a few small strokes in the past
past_medical_history.how_managed: under his GP; on a blood thinner and a cholesterol tablet

past_medical_history.condition_as_patient_states: his speech has been a bit slurred for a long time and he sometimes has a little trouble swallowing — that's his normal

social_history_volunteered.relevant_life_context: lives with spouse; independently mobile at home; has withdrawn from social outings recently because of embarrassment about the crying

communication_style.typical_message_style: spouse speaks for much of the call; the patient interjects, upset and frustrated that he cannot stop crying even though he says he feels fine

objective_data_offered.value: on video he is crying and wiping tears, yet keeps saying he is not upset and does not know why he is crying; he settles and appears calm between the bursts
objective_data_offered.reliability_caveat: observed over a video call, not a clinician examination

objective_data_offered.value: spouse reports the episodes are sudden, come many times a day, are triggered by trivial things, and that he is otherwise his usual self and knows exactly where he is and what is going on
spouse reports no new weakness down one side, no new drooping of the face, and no sudden new problem swallowing beyond his usual mild trouble
objective_data_offered.reliability_caveat: carer observation, not a neurological examination
